graph.label: Fourier Analysis
design_notes: Curated conceptual knowledge graph of Fourier analysis (not tied to a single text).
Nodes are named concepts/results; edges form a prerequisite DAG via `requires`/`instance_of`.
Soft associations live in each node's `related[]` (panel-only, not layout edges).

domains.label: Foundations & Function Spaces
domains.description: Periodic functions, inner-product geometry, and the function spaces (L^1, L^2, Schwartz) where Fourier analysis lives.

domains.label: Fourier Series
domains.description: Decomposing periodic functions into harmonics: coefficients, partial sums, and the basic identities.

domains.label: Convergence & Summability
domains.description: When and how Fourier series converge: kernels, summability methods, and the classical convergence theorems.

domains.label: The Fourier Transform
domains.description: Fourier analysis on the line and R^d: inversion, convolution, Plancherel, and the uncertainty principle.

domains.label: Distributions
domains.description: Generalized functions: tempered distributions, the Dirac delta, and Fourier analysis beyond classical functions.

domains.label: Discrete & Computational
domains.description: Finite and sampled Fourier analysis: the DFT, the FFT, and the sampling theorem.

domains.label: Applications
domains.description: Where it all pays off: PDEs, signal processing, and probability.

domains.label: Dirichlet's Theorem & Number Theory
domains.description: Characters, L-functions, the Euler product, and primes in arithmetic progressions.

items.label: Periodic Function
items.statement: A function whose values repeat after a fixed nonzero period; $2\pi$-periodic functions are functions on the circle.
items.formal_statement: A function $f:\mathbb R\to\mathbb C$ is periodic with period $T>0$ if $f(x+T)=f(x)$ for every $x\in\mathbb R$. In Fourier series, the standard convention is $T=2\pi$, so functions are viewed on $\mathbb T=\mathbb R/2\pi\mathbb Z$.
items.definition: $$\begin{aligned} f:\mathbb R\to\mathbb C,\quad T>0,\quad f(x+T)&=f(x)\quad(\forall x\in\mathbb R),\\ \mathbb T&=\mathbb R/2\pi\mathbb Z. \end{aligned}$$
items.intuition: A periodic function lives on a loop: moving by one full period brings you back to the same value.
assumptions: Periods are taken positive; a $2\pi$-periodic function may be identified with a function on the circle.

items.label: Complex Exponential
items.statement: The pure harmonics $e^{inx}$ that serve as the basic waves in Fourier series.
items.formal_statement: For $n\in\mathbb Z$, define $e_n(x)=e^{inx}$. Euler's formula gives $e^{inx}=\cos(nx)+i\sin(nx)$, and $e_n$ is $2\pi$-periodic.
items.intuition: Each $e_n$ is one pure frequency. Fourier analysis decomposes a signal by measuring how much it aligns with each of these rotating waves.

items.label: Inner Product Space
items.statement: A vector space with a notion of length, angle, and orthogonality.
items.formal_statement: A complex inner product space is a complex vector space $V$ with $\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle:V\times V\to\mathbb C$ that is linear in the first variable, conjugate-linear in the second, conjugate-symmetric, and positive-definite. On $L^2(\mathbb T)$, $\langle f,g\rangle=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}f(x)\overline{g(x)}\,dx$.
items.definition: $$\begin{aligned} \langle ax+by,z\rangle&=a\langle x,z\rangle+b\langle y,z\rangle,\\ \langle x,ay+bz\rangle&=\overline a\langle x,y\rangle+\overline b\langle x,z\rangle,\\ \langle x,y\rangle&=\overline{\langle y,x\rangle},\\ \langle x,x\rangle&\ge0,\\ \langle x,x\rangle=0&\Longleftrightarrow x=0. \end{aligned}$$
items.intuition: This is the geometry behind Fourier coefficients: coefficients are projections, and different frequencies are perpendicular directions.
assumptions: Convention used here: inner products are linear in the first variable and conjugate-linear in the second.

items.label: Orthonormal System
items.statement: A family of unit vectors that are mutually orthogonal.
items.formal_statement: A family $\{\varphi_k\}_{k\in I}$ in an inner product space is orthonormal if $\langle\varphi_j,\varphi_k\rangle=\delta_{jk}$ for all $j,k\in I$. In $L^2(\mathbb T)$, $\{e^{inx}\}_{n\in\mathbb Z}$ is orthonormal.
items.intuition: An orthonormal system gives independent directions. Fourier coefficients are coordinates along those directions.

items.label: L^p Space
items.statement: Equivalence classes of measurable functions whose size is finite in an $L^p$ norm.
items.formal_statement: On $\mathbb T$, for $1\le p<\infty$, $L^p(\mathbb T)=\{f:\|f\|_p=(\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}|f(x)|^p\,dx)^{1/p}<\infty\}$, with functions identified if they agree a.e.; for $p=\infty$, $\|f\|_\infty=\operatorname*{ess\,sup}_{x\in\mathbb T}|f(x)|$.
items.definition: $$\begin{aligned} L^p(\mathbb T)&=\left\{f:\mathbb T\to\mathbb C\ \text{measurable}:\ \|f\|_p<\infty\right\}/\sim,\qquad 1\le p\le\infty,\\ f\sim g&\Longleftrightarrow f=g\ \text{a.e.},\\ \|f\|_p&=\left(\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}|f(x)|^p\,dx\right)^{1/p},\qquad 1\le p<\infty,\\ \|f\|_\infty&=\operatorname*{ess\,sup}_{x\in\mathbb T}|f(x)|. \end{aligned}$$
items.intuition: $L^p$ measures average size. Small $p$ tolerates spikes more easily; large $p$ controls peaks more strongly.
assumptions: Functions are identified up to equality almost everywhere.

items.label: L^1 (Integrable Functions)
items.statement: Absolutely integrable functions on the circle, the minimal setting where Fourier coefficients are defined.
items.formal_statement: $L^1(\mathbb T)=\{f:\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}|f(x)|\,dx<\infty\}$, modulo equality almost everywhere.
items.definition: $$\begin{aligned} L^1(\mathbb T)&=\left\{f:\mathbb T\to\mathbb C\ \text{measurable}:\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}|f(x)|\,dx<\infty\right\}/\sim,\\ f\sim g&\Longleftrightarrow f=g\ \text{a.e.} \end{aligned}$$
items.intuition: Integrability is enough to define $\hat f(n)$. Convergence of the Fourier series requires extra structure.
assumptions: Functions are identified up to equality almost everywhere.

items.label: L^2 (Square-Integrable)
items.statement: Square-integrable functions on the circle, where Fourier series become Hilbert-space geometry.
items.formal_statement: $L^2(\mathbb T)$ consists of measurable functions with $\|f\|_2^2=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}|f(x)|^2\,dx<\infty$, modulo equality a.e. Its inner product is $\langle f,g\rangle=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} f(x)\overline{g(x)}\,dx$.
items.definition: $$\begin{aligned} L^2(\mathbb T)&=\left\{f:\mathbb T\to\mathbb C\ \text{measurable}:\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}|f(x)|^2\,dx<\infty\right\}/\sim,\\ f\sim g&\Longleftrightarrow f=g\ \text{a.e.},\\ \langle f,g\rangle&=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}f(x)\overline{g(x)}\,dx. \end{aligned}$$
items.intuition: In $L^2$, Fourier coefficients are coordinates, partial sums are orthogonal projections, and convergence means convergence in energy.
assumptions: Functions are identified up to equality almost everywhere.

items.label: Hilbert Space
items.statement: A complete inner-product space where orthogonal projection and infinite expansions behave well.
items.formal_statement: A Hilbert space is an inner product space that is complete in the norm $\|x\|=\sqrt{\langle x,x\rangle}$. Central examples are $L^2(\mathbb T)$, $L^2(\mathbb R)$, and $\ell^2(\mathbb Z)$.
items.definition: $$\begin{aligned} \|x\|&=\sqrt{\langle x,x\rangle},\\ \left(\forall\varepsilon>0\ \exists N\ \forall m,n\ge N:\ \|x_m-x_n\|<\varepsilon\right)&\Longrightarrow \exists x\in H:\ \|x_n-x\|\to0. \end{aligned}$$
items.intuition: Completeness means Cauchy sequences of approximations have a limit inside the space, so infinite orthogonal expansions have somewhere to land.

items.label: Schwartz Space
items.statement: Smooth functions whose derivatives decay faster than every polynomial.
items.formal_statement: The Schwartz space $\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$ consists of all $f\in C^\infty(\mathbb R)$ such that $\sup_{x\in\mathbb R}|x^\alpha f^{(\beta)}(x)|<\infty$ for all nonnegative integers $\alpha,\beta$.
items.definition: $$\mathcal S(\mathbb R)=\left\{f\in C^\infty(\mathbb R):\sup_{x\in\mathbb R}|x^\alpha f^{(\beta)}(x)|<\infty\quad(\forall \alpha,\beta\in\mathbb N_0)\right\}.$$
items.intuition: Schwartz functions are the safest playground for the Fourier transform: decay, smoothness, differentiation, and inversion all work cleanly.
assumptions: This one-dimensional definition extends to $\mathbb R^d$ using multi-indices.

items.label: Convolution
items.statement: A sliding weighted average of one function against another.
items.formal_statement: On $\mathbb T$, $(f*g)(x)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}f(y)g(x-y)\,dy$. On $\mathbb R$, $(f*g)(x)=\int_{\mathbb R}f(y)g(x-y)\,dy$, whenever the integral is defined.
items.intuition: Convolution blends nearby values according to a kernel. Fourier analysis is powerful because it turns this averaging operation into multiplication.
assumptions: The normalization differs between the circle and the real line.

items.label: Fourier Coefficient
items.statement: The coordinate of a periodic function at frequency $n$.
items.formal_statement: For $f\in L^1(\mathbb T)$, $\hat f(n)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}f(x)e^{-inx}\,dx=\langle f,e_n\rangle$ when the inner product notation is justified.
items.intuition: The coefficient $\hat f(n)$ measures alignment with the $n$-th rotating wave. Large alignment means that frequency is strongly present.
assumptions: For $L^1$ functions the integral definition is primary; the projection interpretation is geometrically literal in $L^2$.

items.label: Fourier Series
items.statement: The formal reconstruction of a periodic function from all its Fourier coefficients.
items.formal_statement: For $f\in L^1(\mathbb T)$, its Fourier series is written $f(x)\sim\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}\hat f(n)e^{inx}$. The symbol $\sim$ means “has Fourier series,” not automatic pointwise equality.
items.intuition: The series is a candidate reconstruction. Much of Fourier analysis asks in what sense these candidate sums converge back to $f$.
assumptions: The notation $f\sim\sum\hat f(n)e^{inx}$ does not by itself assert convergence.

items.label: Trigonometric Polynomial
items.statement: A finite linear combination of Fourier harmonics.
items.formal_statement: A trigonometric polynomial has the form $p(x)=\sum_{|n|\le N}c_ne^{inx}$ for some $N\ge0$ and complex coefficients $c_n$.
items.intuition: These are finite Fourier series: simple, smooth, periodic building blocks used to approximate more complicated functions.

items.label: Partial Sum
items.statement: The finite Fourier approximation obtained by keeping only frequencies $|n|\le N$.
items.formal_statement: For $f\in L^1(\mathbb T)$, $S_Nf(x)=\sum_{|n|\le N}\hat f(n)e^{inx}$. Equivalently, $S_Nf=D_N*f$, where $D_N$ is the Dirichlet kernel.
items.intuition: A partial sum is the low-frequency shadow of $f$. Whether these shadows converge to the original function is subtle.

items.label: Bessel's Inequality
items.statement: The squared Fourier coefficients of an $L^2$ function cannot carry more energy than the function itself.
items.formal_statement: If $f\in L^2(\mathbb T)$, then $\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}|\hat f(n)|^2\le \|f\|_2^2$.
items.intuition: Projecting onto orthonormal directions cannot create energy. Equality requires that the chosen directions are complete.
assumptions: This is an $L^2$ statement; it uses the normalized inner product on $\mathbb T$.

items.label: Parseval's Identity
items.statement: Fourier coefficients preserve the full $L^2$ energy of a function.
items.formal_statement: For $f,g\in L^2(\mathbb T)$, $\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}|\hat f(n)|^2=\|f\|_2^2$ and $\langle f,g\rangle=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}\hat f(n)\overline{\hat g(n)}$.
items.definition: $$\begin{aligned} f,g\in L^2(\mathbb T)&\Longrightarrow \sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}|\widehat f(n)|^2=\|f\|_2^2,\\ \langle f,g\rangle&=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}\widehat f(n)\overline{\widehat g(n)}. \end{aligned}$$
items.intuition: Fourier analysis is an isometry in $L^2$: functions and their coefficient sequences carry the same geometric information.
assumptions: Equality uses completeness of the trigonometric system in $L^2(\mathbb T)$.

items.label: Riemann–Lebesgue Lemma
items.statement: Fourier coefficients of an integrable function vanish at high frequency.
items.formal_statement: If $f\in L^1(\mathbb T)$, then $\hat f(n)\to0$ as $|n|\to\infty$. Equivalently, high-frequency oscillations average out against every $L^1$ function on the circle.
items.intuition: The waves $e^{-inx}$ oscillate faster and faster. Against an integrable function, their positive and negative swings cancel in the limit.
assumptions: The function is understood as an $L^1(\mathbb T)$ equivalence class; the conclusion concerns its Fourier coefficients.

items.label: Uniqueness Theorem
items.statement: An integrable function is determined, up to equality almost everywhere, by all of its Fourier coefficients.
items.formal_statement: If $f\in L^1(\mathbb T)$ and $\hat f(n)=0$ for every $n\in\mathbb Z$, then $f=0$ a.e. In particular, if $f\in C(\mathbb T)$, then $f\equiv0$.
items.definition: $$f\in L^1(\mathbb T),\quad \widehat f(n)=0\ (\forall n\in\mathbb Z)\Longrightarrow f=0\ \text{a.e.}$$
items.intuition: The full frequency spectrum contains all information about the function. For continuous functions this recovers pointwise equality; for $L^1$ functions equality means equality almost everywhere.
assumptions: For $L^1$ functions, equality is interpreted almost everywhere. For continuous representatives, it becomes pointwise equality.

items.label: Best Approximation Property
items.statement: The Fourier partial sum is the orthogonal projection onto the low-frequency trigonometric polynomials.
items.formal_statement: For $f\in L^2(\mathbb T)$, $S_Nf$ is the unique element of $\operatorname{span}\{e^{inx}:|n|\le N\}$ minimizing $\|f-p\|_2$. Equivalently, $f-S_Nf$ is orthogonal to every trigonometric polynomial of degree at most $N$.
items.intuition: Truncating a Fourier series is not a random cutoff. It is the closest low-frequency shadow of $f$ in the geometry of $L^2$.
assumptions: The minimization is in the $L^2$-norm on $\mathbb T$.

items.label: Decay–Smoothness Duality
items.statement: Smoothness of a periodic function forces decay of its Fourier coefficients; sufficiently fast coefficient decay forces smoothness.
items.formal_statement: If $f$ is $2\pi$-periodic and $C^k$ with matching periodic derivatives up to order $k$, then repeated integration by parts gives $\hat f(n)=o(|n|^{-k})$ under standard endpoint regularity. Conversely, if $\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}|n|^k|\hat f(n)|<\infty$, then the Fourier series defines a $C^k$ function.
items.definition: $$\begin{aligned} f\in C^k(\mathbb T)&\Longrightarrow \widehat f(n)=o(|n|^{-k})\quad(|n|\to\infty),\\ \sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}|n|^k|\widehat f(n)|<\infty&\Longrightarrow \sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}\widehat f(n)e^{inx}\in C^k(\mathbb T). \end{aligned}$$
items.intuition: Differentiation multiplies the $n$-th Fourier coefficient by $in$. Smooth functions therefore have coefficients that die quickly, while sufficiently fast decay lets derivatives be reconstructed term by term.
assumptions: Periodic endpoint matching matters: derivatives are taken on the circle, not merely on an interval with unrelated endpoints.

items.label: Good Kernel (Approximate Identity)
items.statement: A normalized family of kernels whose mass concentrates near $0$, so convolution approximates the identity.
items.formal_statement: A family $\{K_n\}$ on $\mathbb T$ is a good kernel if $\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}K_n(x)\,dx=1$, $\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}|K_n(x)|\,dx\le M$, and for every $\delta>0$, $\frac1{2\pi}\int_{\delta\le |x|\le\pi}|K_n(x)|\,dx\to0$. Then $K_n*f\to f$ uniformly for $f\in C(\mathbb T)$.
items.intuition: A good kernel is a controlled bump whose mass collapses to the origin. Convolution replaces $f(x)$ by a weighted local average near $x$, and the average becomes exact in the limit.
assumptions: Kernels are normalized using the circle measure $\frac1{2\pi}dx$.
items.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.2 Theorem 4.1; Ch.5 Corollary 1.7; Ch.5 Theorem 1.6

items.label: Dirichlet Kernel
items.statement: The convolution kernel for ordinary Fourier partial sums.
items.formal_statement: $D_N(x)=\sum_{|n|\le N}e^{inx}=\frac{\sin((N+\frac12)x)}{\sin(x/2)}$, with removable value $D_N(0)=2N+1$. For $f\in L^1(\mathbb T)$, $S_Nf=D_N*f$.
items.definition: $$\begin{aligned} D_N(x)&=\sum_{|n|\le N}e^{inx},\\ D_N(x)&=\frac{\sin((N+\frac12)x)}{\sin(x/2)},\qquad x\notin2\pi\mathbb Z,\\ D_N(2\pi k)&=2N+1,\qquad k\in\mathbb Z. \end{aligned}$$
items.intuition: $D_N$ reconstructs by sharp frequency cutoff. Its oscillations and growing $L^1$-norm make ordinary partial sums unstable compared with Fejér averages.

items.label: Fejér Kernel
items.statement: The nonnegative Cesàro average of Dirichlet kernels.
items.formal_statement: $F_N(x)=\frac1{N+1}\sum_{n=0}^{N}D_n(x)=\frac1{N+1}\left(\frac{\sin((N+1)x/2)}{\sin(x/2)}\right)^2\ge0$, with $F_N(0)=N+1$, and $\sigma_Nf=F_N*f$.
items.definition: $$\begin{aligned} F_N(x)&=\frac1{N+1}\sum_{n=0}^{N}D_n(x),\\ F_N(x)&=\frac1{N+1}\left(\frac{\sin((N+1)x/2)}{\sin(x/2)}\right)^2,\qquad x\notin2\pi\mathbb Z,\\ F_N(2\pi k)&=N+1,\qquad k\in\mathbb Z. \end{aligned}$$
items.intuition: Averaging the unstable Dirichlet kernels cancels their worst oscillations. Positivity and mass concentration make $F_N$ a genuine approximate identity.

items.label: Poisson Kernel
items.statement: The positive approximate identity behind Abel summation and harmonic extension to the disk.
items.formal_statement: For $0\le r<1$, $P_r(\theta)=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}r^{|n|}e^{in\theta}=\frac{1-r^2}{1-2r\cos\theta+r^2}$, with $P_r\ge0$ and $\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}P_r(\theta)\,d\theta=1$. As $r\to1^-$, $P_r*f\to f$ for $f\in C(\mathbb T)$.
items.intuition: $P_r*f$ is the harmonic extension of boundary data into the disk. Letting $r\to1^-$ approaches the boundary from inside, smoothing first and recovering later.

items.label: Cesàro Summation
items.statement: A summability process that replaces partial sums by their arithmetic averages.
items.formal_statement: For Fourier series, $\sigma_Nf(x)=\frac1{N+1}\sum_{n=0}^{N}S_nf(x)$. Equivalently, $\sigma_Nf=F_N*f$.
items.intuition: Instead of trusting the last partial sum, Cesàro summation averages all earlier partial sums. This damps oscillation and leads to Fejér's theorem.

items.label: Abel Summation
items.statement: A summability process that damps high frequencies by $r^{|n|}$, then lets $r\to1^-$.
items.formal_statement: For a Fourier series $\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}\hat f(n)e^{inx}$, its Abel means are $A_rf(x)=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}r^{|n|}\hat f(n)e^{inx}$, $0\le r<1$. Abel summability asks whether $A_rf(x)\to L$ as $r\to1^-$.
items.intuition: Abel summation approaches the Fourier series from inside the unit disk. The factor $r^{|n|}$ suppresses high frequencies first, then gradually releases them.

items.label: Fejér's Theorem
items.statement: Fejer means of a continuous periodic function converge uniformly to the function.
items.formal_statement: If $f\in C(\mathbb T)$, then $\sigma_Nf=F_N*f\to f$ uniformly on $\mathbb T$. More generally, if $f\in L^1(\mathbb T)$, then $\sigma_Nf\to f$ in $L^1$.
items.intuition: Averaging partial sums replaces the oscillatory Dirichlet kernel by the positive Fejer kernel. The result is a stable local average that sharpens into $f$.
assumptions: Uniform convergence requires $f\in C(\mathbb T)$; the $L^1$ version has $L^1$-convergence.

items.label: Mean-Square Convergence
items.statement: Fourier partial sums converge to every $L^2$ function in mean square.
items.formal_statement: For every $f\in L^2(\mathbb T)$, $\|f-S_Nf\|_2\to0$ as $N\to\infty$. Equivalently, the trigonometric system $\{e^{inx}\}_{n\in\mathbb Z}$ is complete in $L^2(\mathbb T)$.
items.intuition: In $L^2$, Fourier series are orthogonal projections onto larger and larger frequency subspaces. Completeness says those shadows converge back to the original object in energy.
assumptions: Convergence is in the $L^2$-norm, not necessarily pointwise.

items.label: Pointwise Convergence Problem
items.statement: The question whether Fourier partial sums converge at a specific point.
items.formal_statement: Given $f\in L^1(\mathbb T)$ and $x_0\in\mathbb T$, ask whether $S_Nf(x_0)=(D_N*f)(x_0)$ has a limit, and whether that limit equals $f(x_0)$ or the jump average $\frac12(f(x_0^+)+f(x_0^-))$.
items.intuition: Pointwise convergence is delicate because the Dirichlet kernel is oscillatory and not a good approximate identity. Local regularity can tame it; mere continuity cannot.
assumptions: The value $f(x_0)$ must be meaningful; for discontinuities one usually compares with the average of one-sided limits.

items.label: Dini's Criterion
items.statement: A local Dini condition guarantees pointwise convergence at a chosen point.
items.formal_statement: If $f\in L^1(\mathbb T)$ and $\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}\left|\frac{f(x_0+t)-f(x_0)}{t}\right|\,dt<\infty$, then $S_Nf(x_0)\to f(x_0)$.
items.intuition: The condition measures whether the local error $f(x_0+t)-f(x_0)$ is tame enough to survive division by $t$. It is a pointwise regularity test, not a global smoothness assumption.
assumptions: The value $f(x_0)$ must be specified; the condition is local at $x_0$.

items.label: Dirichlet's Convergence Theorem
items.statement: Piecewise smooth functions have Fourier partial sums converging to the midpoint of each jump.
items.formal_statement: If $f$ is $2\pi$-periodic and piecewise $C^1$, then $S_Nf(x)\to\frac12(f(x^+)+f(x^-))$ at every $x$. In particular, at points of continuity, $S_Nf(x)\to f(x)$.
items.intuition: Fourier series cannot choose one side of a jump, so the symmetric oscillations settle on the average of the two one-sided limits.
assumptions: Uses a periodic piecewise $C^1$ convention with one-sided limits at jumps.

items.label: Gibbs Phenomenon
items.statement: Fourier partial sums have a persistent overshoot near jump discontinuities.
items.formal_statement: For a piecewise smooth function with a jump of size $J$, the overshoot of $S_Nf$ near the jump approaches $GJ$, where $G=\frac1\pi\int_0^\pi\frac{\sin t}{t}\,dt-\frac12\approx0.08949$. The overshoot region narrows as $N\to\infty$, but its height does not vanish.
items.intuition: Truncating frequencies cannot draw a sharp jump cleanly. The ringing gets squeezed toward the discontinuity, but the first overshoot keeps roughly nine percent of the jump.
assumptions: The standard statement concerns Fourier partial sums near a jump of a piecewise smooth function.

items.label: Carleson's Theorem
items.statement: Fourier series of $L^2$ functions converge almost everywhere.
items.formal_statement: If $f\in L^2(\mathbb T)$, then $S_Nf(x)\to f(x)$ for almost every $x\in\mathbb T$. Hunt extended this to $L^p(\mathbb T)$ for $1<p<\infty$.
items.intuition: Despite bad kernels and possible pointwise chaos, square-integrability is still strong enough to force convergence at almost every point. The proof is deep; the statement is not a routine kernel argument.
assumptions: Almost everywhere is with respect to Lebesgue measure on $\mathbb T$.

items.label: Du Bois-Reymond Counterexample
items.statement: There exists a continuous periodic function whose Fourier partial sums diverge at a point.
items.formal_statement: There is $f\in C(\mathbb T)$ such that the sequence $S_Nf(x_0)$ diverges for some $x_0\in\mathbb T$; in stronger versions, $\sup_N|S_Nf(x_0)|=\infty$.
items.intuition: Continuity alone does not control the Dirichlet kernel strongly enough. This counterexample explains why Fejer averaging is not just a cosmetic improvement.
assumptions: The counterexample concerns ordinary Fourier partial sums, not Cesaro or Abel means.

items.label: Fourier Transform
items.statement: The transform that decomposes functions on $\mathbb R$ into continuous frequencies.
items.formal_statement: For $f\in L^1(\mathbb R)$, $\hat f(\xi)=\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,dx$. This graph uses the $2\pi$-frequency convention.
items.intuition: Fourier coefficients measure alignment with discrete waves on the circle; the Fourier transform does the same with a continuum of waves on the line.
assumptions: Convention: $\hat f(\xi)=\int f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,dx$.

items.label: Fourier Inversion Theorem
items.statement: The Fourier transform is inverted by the opposite-sign integral when the hypotheses justify reconstruction.
items.formal_statement: For $f\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$, $f(x)=\int_{\mathbb R}\hat f(\xi)e^{2\pi i x\xi}\,d\xi$. More generally, inversion holds in other spaces with the appropriate convergence interpretation.
items.definition: $$f\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)\Longrightarrow f(x)=\int_{\mathbb R}\widehat f(\xi)e^{2\pi i x\xi}\,d\xi.$$
items.intuition: Analysis and synthesis are symmetric: the inverse transform recombines continuous waves with the sign reversed.
assumptions: Uses the graph's $2\pi$-frequency convention.

items.label: Convolution Theorem
items.statement: The Fourier transform turns convolution into multiplication, and multiplication into convolution under suitable hypotheses.
items.formal_statement: If $f,g\in L^1(\mathbb R)$, then $\widehat{f*g}=\hat f\,\hat g$. If $f,g\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$, then $\widehat{fg}=\hat f*\hat g$, using the same Fourier convention.
items.intuition: Fourier waves diagonalize translation-invariant averaging. That is why convolution, which mixes nearby values, becomes pointwise multiplication in frequency.
assumptions: Assumptions depend on the identity: $L^1$ is enough for convolution-to-product; Schwartz functions safely justify both identities.

items.label: Plancherel's Theorem
items.statement: The Fourier transform extends to a unitary operator on $L^2(\mathbb R)$.
items.formal_statement: For $f\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$, $\|\hat f\|_{L^2(\mathbb R)}=\|f\|_{L^2(\mathbb R)}$. Hence $\mathcal F$ extends uniquely to a unitary map on $L^2(\mathbb R)$.
items.definition: $$\begin{aligned} \mathcal F:L^2(\mathbb R)&\to L^2(\mathbb R),\\ \|\widehat f\|_2&=\|f\|_2,\\ \langle \widehat f,\widehat g\rangle&=\langle f,g\rangle. \end{aligned}$$
items.intuition: The Fourier transform preserves energy. In $L^2$, it is not a lossy change of representation but a rotation of Hilbert space.
assumptions: Fourier convention $e^{-2\pi i x\xi}$; $L^2$ is on $\mathbb R$.

items.label: Gaussian (Self-Dual)
items.statement: The normalized Gaussian is fixed by the Fourier transform.
items.formal_statement: With the graph's convention, if $f(x)=e^{-\pi x^2}$, then $\hat f(\xi)=e^{-\pi \xi^2}$. More generally, Fourier transforms send Gaussians to Gaussians.
items.intuition: The Gaussian is the perfectly balanced function: stable under Fourier transform and extremal for uncertainty.
assumptions: Uses the $e^{-2\pi i x\xi}$ convention.

items.label: Uncertainty Principle
items.statement: A nonzero function and its Fourier transform cannot both be arbitrarily concentrated.
items.formal_statement: If $f\in L^2(\mathbb R)$, $xf\in L^2$, $\xi\hat f\in L^2$, and $\|f\|_2=1$, then $\Delta_x(f)\Delta_\xi(\hat f)\ge \frac1{4\pi}$, where $\Delta_x,\Delta_\xi$ are centered standard deviations in position and frequency.
items.intuition: Narrow localization in $x$ requires many frequencies; narrow localization in frequency forces spreading in $x$. Gaussians are the optimally balanced case.
assumptions: Uses centered variances; equality occurs for Gaussian wave packets under the $2\pi$-frequency convention.

items.label: Poisson Summation Formula
items.statement: Periodizing a function in space corresponds to sampling its Fourier transform.
items.formal_statement: For $f\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$, using $\hat f(\xi)=\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,dx$, one has $\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}f(n)=\sum_{k\in\mathbb Z}\hat f(k)$.
items.intuition: The formula is the hinge between continuous and discrete Fourier analysis: adding copies of $f$ at integer shifts turns into reading $\hat f$ at integer frequencies.
assumptions: Stated here for Schwartz functions to avoid convergence side quests. Other versions require explicit decay and regularity hypotheses.

items.label: Fourier Transform on R^d
items.statement: The Fourier transform on $\mathbb R^d$, decomposing functions into multidimensional plane waves.
items.formal_statement: For $f\in L^1(\mathbb R^d)$, $\hat f(\xi)=\int_{\mathbb R^d}f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\cdot\xi}\,dx$, where $x,\xi\in\mathbb R^d$. The inverse uses $e^{2\pi i x\cdot\xi}$ when inversion is valid.
items.definition: $$\widehat f(\xi)=\int_{\mathbb R^d}f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\cdot\xi}\,dx,\qquad x,\xi\in\mathbb R^d.$$
items.intuition: In several variables, the pure frequencies are plane waves. The vector $\xi$ records both direction and rate of oscillation.
assumptions: Uses the same $2\pi$-frequency convention as the one-dimensional transform.
items.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.6 Corollary 2.2; Ch.6 Corollary 2.3; Ch.6 Proposition 2.1

metadata.source: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.6 Corollary 2.2; Ch.6 Corollary 2.3; Ch.6 Proposition 2.1

items.label: Differentiation Rule
items.statement: Differentiation becomes multiplication by frequency under the Fourier transform.
items.formal_statement: For $f\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$, $\widehat{f'}(\xi)=2\pi i\xi\,\hat f(\xi)$ and $\widehat{xf}(\xi)=-\frac1{2\pi i}\frac{d}{d\xi}\hat f(\xi)$.
items.intuition: The Fourier transform turns differential operators into algebraic multipliers, which is why linear constant-coefficient PDEs become solvable in frequency space.
assumptions: Schwartz hypotheses avoid boundary terms and justify integration by parts.

items.label: Tempered Distribution
items.statement: A continuous linear functional on Schwartz space.
items.formal_statement: A tempered distribution is an element $T\in\mathcal S'(\mathbb R)$, the continuous dual of $\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$. A locally integrable function of at most polynomial growth defines one by $T_f(\varphi)=\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)\varphi(x)\,dx$.
items.definition: $$\mathcal S'(\mathbb R)=\mathcal L_{\mathrm{cont}}(\mathcal S(\mathbb R),\mathbb C).$$
items.intuition: Tempered distributions let Fourier analysis handle objects such as constants, deltas, and polynomials that are not ordinary integrable functions.
assumptions: Continuity is with respect to the Schwartz-space topology.

items.label: Dirac Delta
items.statement: The unit point mass at the origin, acting by evaluation.
items.formal_statement: The Dirac delta is the distribution $\delta(\varphi)=\varphi(0)$. In distributional notation, $\delta*T=T$ when convolution is defined, and $\hat\delta=1$ under the graph's Fourier convention.
items.intuition: The delta is not an ordinary function. It is the idealized point source and the limiting shape of approximate identities.
assumptions: Convolution and Fourier transform identities are interpreted distributionally.

items.label: Weak Derivative
items.statement: A distributional derivative is defined by transferring the derivative to the test function.
items.formal_statement: For a distribution $T$, its derivative $T'$ is defined by $T'(\varphi)=-T(\varphi')$ for every test function $\varphi$. More generally, $T^{(k)}(\varphi)=(-1)^kT(\varphi^{(k)})$.
items.intuition: Integration by parts moves derivatives off rough objects and onto smooth test functions. This lets jumps and spikes have derivatives in a generalized sense.

items.label: Fourier Transform of Distributions
items.statement: The Fourier transform extends from functions to tempered distributions by duality.
items.formal_statement: For $T\in\mathcal S'(\mathbb R)$, define $\hat T\in\mathcal S'(\mathbb R)$ by $\hat T(\varphi)=T(\hat\varphi)$ for every $\varphi\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$, using the graph's Fourier convention.
items.intuition: Instead of transforming $T$ directly, test it against transformed test functions. This makes objects like constants, deltas, and polynomials Fourier-transformable.
assumptions: Uses bilinear distribution pairing and the $e^{-2\pi i x\xi}$ convention.

items.label: Roots of Unity
items.statement: The $N$-th roots of unity are the finite phases used as discrete harmonics.
items.formal_statement: $\omega_N=e^{2\pi i/N}$, and $\omega_N^k$, $k=0,\dots,N-1$, are exactly the solutions of $z^N=1$. Characters of $\mathbb Z/N\mathbb Z$ have the form $k\mapsto\omega_N^{jk}$.
items.intuition: These are the equally spaced points on the unit circle. They are the finite analogue of the continuous waves $e^{inx}$.

items.label: Cyclic Group
items.statement: A group generated by one element; the model group for length-$N$ finite Fourier analysis.
items.formal_statement: $\mathbb Z/N\mathbb Z$ is the additive group of residue classes modulo $N$. Every element is generated by repeatedly adding $1$.
items.intuition: The cyclic group is a finite clock: after $N$ steps you return to the start. Its characters are the finite waves used by the DFT.

items.label: Finite Abelian Group
items.statement: A finite group whose operation is commutative.
items.formal_statement: A finite abelian group $G$ is a finite set with an associative operation, identity, inverses, and $xy=yx$ for all $x,y\in G$.
items.intuition: Finite abelian groups are the natural finite domains where Fourier analysis works cleanly: the frequencies are group characters.

items.label: Group Character
items.statement: A multiplicative homomorphism from a group into the unit circle.
items.formal_statement: For a finite abelian group $G$, a character is a homomorphism $\chi:G\to \mathbb C^\times$. Since $G$ is finite, $\chi(x)$ is a root of unity and $|\chi(x)|=1$.
items.intuition: A character is a pure frequency on a group. It turns the group operation into multiplication of phases.

items.label: Fourier Analysis on Z(N)
items.statement: Fourier analysis on the cyclic group $\mathbb Z/N\mathbb Z$, where all sums are finite.
items.formal_statement: For functions $f:\mathbb Z/N\mathbb Z\to\mathbb C$, the characters $\chi_j(k)=\omega_N^{jk}$, $j=0,\dots,N-1$, form an orthogonal basis of $L^2(\mathbb Z/N\mathbb Z)$ with normalized inner product $\langle f,g\rangle=\frac1N\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}f(k)\overline{g(k)}$.
items.intuition: This is Fourier series with no convergence trouble: finite data decomposes exactly into finite cyclic waves.
assumptions: The normalized inner product uses the factor $1/N$.

items.label: Discrete Fourier Transform
items.statement: The Fourier transform of a length-$N$ vector.
items.formal_statement: For $a=(a_0,\dots,a_{N-1})$, $\hat a(k)=\sum_{j=0}^{N-1}a_j\omega_N^{-jk}$, $k=0,\dots,N-1$, where $\omega_N=e^{2\pi i/N}$.
items.intuition: The DFT decomposes finite data into finite cyclic frequencies. It is exact linear algebra, not an approximation by itself.
assumptions: Normalization conventions vary; this graph puts the $1/N$ factor in the inverse transform.

items.label: Discrete-Time Fourier Transform
items.statement: The Fourier transform of an infinite sequence, producing a periodic frequency function.
items.formal_statement: For $a\in\ell^1(\mathbb Z)$, $\hat a(\xi)=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}a_n e^{-2\pi i n\xi}$, and $\hat a$ is $1$-periodic in $\xi$.
items.definition: $$\widehat a(\xi)=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}a_ne^{-2\pi i n\xi},\qquad \xi\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z.$$
items.intuition: Discrete time creates periodic frequency. This is the dual picture to Fourier series.
assumptions: Absolute summability gives a continuous periodic transform; weaker sequence spaces need weaker interpretations.

items.label: Fast Fourier Transform
items.statement: An $O(N\log N)$ algorithm for computing the DFT.
items.formal_statement: For composite $N$, Cooley-Tukey factorizes a size-$N$ DFT into smaller DFTs. For $N=2^m$, this gives $O(N\log N)$ operations instead of the direct $O(N^2)$ computation.
items.intuition: The FFT exploits symmetry and repeated roots of unity, avoiding the waste of recomputing the same oscillatory patterns.
assumptions: The cleanest radix-2 statement assumes $N$ is a power of two; other factorizations handle other composite sizes.

items.label: Circular Convolution
items.statement: Convolution on $\mathbb Z/N\mathbb Z$, where indices wrap around modulo $N$.
items.formal_statement: For $a,b:\mathbb Z/N\mathbb Z\to\mathbb C$, define $(a\circledast b)_k=\sum_{j=0}^{N-1}a_j b_{k-j\,\mathrm{mod}\,N}$. With the unnormalized DFT $\hat a(k)=\sum_j a_j\omega_N^{-jk}$, one has $\widehat{a\circledast b}(k)=\hat a(k)\hat b(k)$.
items.intuition: Circular convolution is sliding-and-averaging on a finite clock. The DFT turns this wrapped mixing operation into pointwise multiplication.
assumptions: The exact constants depend on the DFT normalization. Here the DFT is unnormalized and the inverse carries the factor $1/N$.

items.label: Nyquist–Shannon Sampling
items.statement: A band-limited function can be reconstructed from sufficiently dense samples.
items.formal_statement: If $f\in L^2(\mathbb R)$ and $\operatorname{supp}\hat f\subset[-W,W]$, then $f$ is recovered from samples spaced $1/(2W)$ by $f(x)=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}f(\frac{n}{2W})\operatorname{sinc}(2Wx-n)$, with convergence in $L^2$ and, under stronger regularity, pointwise or uniform convergence. Here $\operatorname{sinc}(t)=\frac{\sin(\pi t)}{\pi t}$.
items.intuition: Band limitation means there are no hidden high-frequency wiggles between samples. Sampling above the Nyquist rate captures all degrees of freedom.
assumptions: Band-limited in the graph's Fourier-transform convention; convergence mode depends on regularity.

items.label: Aliasing
items.statement: Undersampling makes different frequencies become indistinguishable.
items.formal_statement: Sampling at rate $f_s$ identifies frequencies modulo $f_s$. Components outside the Nyquist band $[-f_s/2,f_s/2]$ fold back into that band.
items.intuition: Aliasing is not noise; it is ambiguity caused by too few samples. High frequencies impersonate lower ones because the sampled values match.
assumptions: The exact folding description uses ideal uniform sampling.

items.label: Heat Equation
items.statement: Diffusion is solved by damping each Fourier mode.
items.formal_statement: On the circle, $u_t=u_{xx}$, $u(x,0)=f(x)$, has formal solution $u(x,t)=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}\hat f(n)e^{-n^2t}e^{inx}$. On $\mathbb R$, $u(x,t)=(H_t*f)(x)$.
items.intuition: High frequencies decay faster than low frequencies, so heat flow smooths rough data as time increases.
assumptions: Constants depend on whether the circle basis is $e^{inx}$ or the real-line transform convention is used.
items.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.5 Corollary 2.2; Ch.5 Theorem 2.1; Ch.5 Theorem 2.3

metadata.source: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.5 Corollary 2.2; Ch.5 Theorem 2.1; Ch.5 Theorem 2.3

items.label: Wave Equation
items.statement: Vibrating strings decompose into standing harmonic waves.
items.formal_statement: For fixed-end data on $[0,\pi]$, formal solutions have the form $u(x,t)=\sum_{n\ge1}(a_n\cos nt+b_n\sin nt)\sin(nx)$. Periodic formulations use complex exponentials instead.
items.intuition: The wave equation preserves oscillation rather than damping it. Each spatial harmonic evolves as a harmonic oscillator in time.
assumptions: Boundary conditions determine whether sine series, cosine series, or complex Fourier series are appropriate.

items.label: Isoperimetric Inequality
items.statement: A simple closed plane curve of fixed length encloses maximal area exactly when it is a circle.
items.formal_statement: If a sufficiently smooth simple closed plane curve has length $L$ and encloses area $A$, then $A\le L^2/(4\pi)$, with equality exactly for circles.
items.intuition: Writing the boundary curve as a Fourier series turns area and length into coefficient sums. Parseval then becomes a geometric inequality.
assumptions: Assume a regular simple closed curve, for example piecewise $C^1$, oriented positively.

items.label: Weyl Equidistribution
items.statement: Equidistribution can be tested by checking the decay of all nonzero exponential averages.
items.formal_statement: A sequence $(x_n)$ in $\mathbb R/\mathbb Z$ is equidistributed iff $\frac1N\sum_{n=1}^N e^{2\pi i kx_n}\to0$ for every nonzero $k\in\mathbb Z$. In particular, $x_n=n\alpha$ is equidistributed when $\alpha\notin\mathbb Q$.
items.intuition: Intervals can be approximated by trigonometric polynomials, so uniform distribution of points is equivalent to cancellation against every nonzero frequency.
assumptions: The criterion is stated on the circle $\mathbb R/\mathbb Z$.
items.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.4 Corollary 2.3; Ch.4 Lemma 2.2; Ch.4 Theorem 2.1

metadata.source: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.4 Corollary 2.3; Ch.4 Lemma 2.2; Ch.4 Theorem 2.1

items.label: Central Limit Theorem (Fourier proof)
items.statement: Fourier methods prove that normalized sums of independent variables converge in distribution to a Gaussian.
items.formal_statement: For i.i.d. random variables with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2>0$, the characteristic functions of $(X_1+\cdots+X_n-n\mu)/(\sigma\sqrt n)$ converge pointwise to $e^{-t^2/2}$, the characteristic function of the standard normal law.
items.intuition: Independence turns sums into products of characteristic functions. After normalization, those products converge to the Gaussian multiplier.
assumptions: Uses the probability convention $\varphi_X(t)=\mathbb E e^{itX}$, not the graph's $2\pi$-Fourier transform convention.

items.label: Filtering & Signal Processing
items.statement: Frequency-selective processing by convolution in time or space.
items.formal_statement: For a linear time-invariant filter with impulse response $h$, $y=h*x$, so $\hat y=\hat h\,\hat x$ whenever the transforms are defined. The multiplier $\hat h$ is the transfer function.
items.intuition: Filtering is easier in frequency: convolutional systems become pointwise multiplication, so passing or rejecting frequency bands becomes a multiplier design problem.
assumptions: Precise hypotheses depend on whether signals are modeled in $L^1$, $L^2$, distributions, or discrete spaces.

items.label: Primes in Arithmetic Progressions
items.statement: Every reduced arithmetic progression contains infinitely many primes.
items.formal_statement: If $q\ge2$ and $\gcd(a,q)=1$, then infinitely many primes $p$ satisfy $p\equiv a\pmod q$. The proof expands residue-class indicators using Dirichlet characters and uses $L(1,\chi)\ne0$ for nonprincipal characters.
items.intuition: Finite Fourier analysis separates residue classes; analytic information about the resulting $L$-functions prevents any reduced class from running out of primes.
assumptions: The condition $\gcd(a,q)=1$ is necessary: otherwise every number in the progression shares a fixed divisor with $q$.

items.label: Lebesgue Integral
items.statement: The integration theory underlying $L^p$ spaces and modern Fourier analysis.
items.formal_statement: The Lebesgue integral $\int f\,d\mu$ is defined for measurable functions on a measure space. It has strong limit theorems, such as dominated convergence and monotone convergence, that make $L^p$ spaces complete.
items.intuition: Lebesgue integration measures functions by size on sets rather than by partitions of the domain. That is what makes limits of functions behave properly.

items.label: Almost Everywhere
items.statement: A property that fails only on a set of measure zero.
items.formal_statement: A property $P(x)$ holds almost everywhere if $\mu(\{x:\neg P(x)\})=0$. In $L^p$ spaces, functions that agree almost everywhere represent the same element.
items.intuition: Fourier analysis often ignores changes on negligible sets. This is why $L^p$ objects are equivalence classes, not pointwise functions.

items.label: Dominated Convergence
items.statement: A theorem allowing limits to pass through integrals under an integrable bound.
items.formal_statement: If $f_n\to f$ almost everywhere and $|f_n|\le g$ for all $n$, with $g\in L^1$, then $f\in L^1$ and $\int f_n\to\int f$.
items.intuition: A pointwise limit is safe to integrate when all functions stay under one integrable roof.

items.label: Fubini–Tonelli Theorem
items.statement: Theorems that justify changing the order of integration.
items.formal_statement: Tonelli applies to nonnegative measurable functions; Fubini applies when $f\in L^1(X\times Y)$. Under these hypotheses, iterated integrals agree with the integral over the product space.
items.intuition: Convolution, Fourier inversion, and many kernel identities require moving integrals past each other. Fubini and Tonelli say when that move is legal.
assumptions: Tonelli handles nonnegative functions; Fubini handles absolutely integrable functions.

items.label: Real (Sine–Cosine) Series
items.statement: The sine-cosine form of a Fourier series, equivalent to the complex exponential form.
items.formal_statement: For a real-valued $2\pi$-periodic $f$, $f(x)\sim\frac{a_0}{2}+\sum_{n\ge1}(a_n\cos nx+b_n\sin nx)$, where $a_n=\frac1\pi\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}f(x)\cos(nx)\,dx$ and $b_n=\frac1\pi\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}f(x)\sin(nx)\,dx$.
items.intuition: This is the classical form. The complex exponential notation packages the same information more compactly.
assumptions: The displayed coefficients use the $2\pi$-periodic interval $[-\pi,\pi]$.

items.label: Half-Range Expansions
items.statement: A sine or cosine expansion on an interval obtained by odd or even periodic extension.
items.formal_statement: A function on $[0,\pi]$ can be extended oddly to obtain a sine series or evenly to obtain a cosine series on $[-\pi,\pi]$. The chosen extension determines the boundary behavior.
items.intuition: Boundary conditions choose the symmetry. Fixed endpoints naturally lead to sines; Neumann-type symmetry naturally leads to cosines.
assumptions: Endpoint behavior depends on the chosen extension and the regularity of the original function.

items.label: Riemann Localization Principle
items.statement: Pointwise convergence of a Fourier series at a point depends only on the function near that point.
items.formal_statement: If $f,g\in L^1(\mathbb T)$ agree on an open neighborhood of $x_0$, then $S_Nf(x_0)-S_Ng(x_0)\to0$. Hence convergence and the limiting value at $x_0$, when they exist, are local.
items.intuition: Fourier coefficients are global, but the oscillation of the Dirichlet kernel cancels distant contributions at a fixed point.
assumptions: Usually stated for $L^1$ functions on the circle.

items.label: Term-by-Term Differentiation
items.statement: A Fourier series may be differentiated term by term when the differentiated series is sufficiently controlled.
items.formal_statement: If $\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}|n\hat f(n)|<\infty$, then $\sum_n\hat f(n)e^{inx}$ defines a $C^1$ function and may be differentiated term by term: $f'(x)=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}in\hat f(n)e^{inx}$, uniformly in $x$.
items.definition: $$\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}|n\widehat f(n)|<\infty\Longrightarrow \frac{d}{dx}\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}\widehat f(n)e^{inx}=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}in\widehat f(n)e^{inx}.$$
items.intuition: Differentiating a frequency $e^{inx}$ multiplies it by $in$. Absolute summability after that multiplication is the safety check that prevents termwise differentiation from lying to you.
assumptions: The displayed sufficient condition is stronger than necessary but clean and safe.

items.label: Weierstrass Approximation
items.statement: Continuous periodic functions can be uniformly approximated by trigonometric polynomials.
items.formal_statement: For every $f\in C(\mathbb T)$ and every $\varepsilon>0$, there exists a trigonometric polynomial $p$ such that $\|f-p\|_\infty<\varepsilon$. Equivalently, trigonometric polynomials are dense in $C(\mathbb T)$ in the uniform norm.
items.intuition: Fejer means are themselves trigonometric polynomials and converge uniformly to $f$, so they provide explicit approximants rather than just existence magic.
assumptions: This is the trigonometric version on the circle. Algebraic polynomial approximation on intervals is a related but different statement.

items.label: Abel's Theorem
items.statement: Ordinary convergence implies Abel summability to the same value.
items.formal_statement: If $\sum_{n=0}^\infty a_n=s$ converges, then $\lim_{r\to1^-}\sum_{n=0}^\infty a_nr^n=s$. For Fourier series, Abel means agree with the ordinary limit wherever ordinary convergence is already valid.
items.intuition: Abel summation is consistent: the gentler radial limiting process does not change answers that already converge honestly.
assumptions: The displayed scalar form is one-sided in $r$, with $0\le r<1$.

items.label: Translation & Modulation
items.statement: Translation in space becomes modulation in frequency, and modulation in space becomes translation in frequency.
items.formal_statement: For suitable $f$, $\widehat{f(\cdot-a)}(\xi)=e^{-2\pi ia\xi}\hat f(\xi)$ and $\widehat{e^{2\pi ibx}f(x)}(\xi)=\hat f(\xi-b)$.
items.intuition: Moving a signal changes phases but not frequency magnitudes; multiplying by a pure wave shifts the frequency content.
assumptions: Uses the graph's $2\pi$-frequency convention.

items.label: Transform of L^1 is Continuous & Decays
items.statement: The Fourier transform of an integrable function is continuous and vanishes at infinity.
items.formal_statement: If $f\in L^1(\mathbb R)$, then $\hat f\in C_0(\mathbb R)$: it is continuous and $\hat f(\xi)\to0$ as $|\xi|\to\infty$.
items.definition: $$f\in L^1(\mathbb R)\Longrightarrow \widehat f\in C_0(\mathbb R),\qquad \lim_{|\xi|\to\infty}\widehat f(\xi)=0.$$
items.intuition: Integrability makes high-frequency oscillations average out. This is the Riemann-Lebesgue lemma on the line.

items.label: Band-Limited Function
items.statement: A function whose Fourier transform vanishes outside a bounded frequency band.
items.formal_statement: A function $f$ is band-limited to $[-W,W]$ if $\operatorname{supp}\hat f\subset[-W,W]$. Equivalently, no frequencies with $|\xi|>W$ are present.
items.intuition: Band-limited signals have finite frequency range. That frequency restriction makes exact sampling and analytic continuation possible.
assumptions: Support of $\hat f$ is understood in the appropriate sense: ordinary support for continuous transforms, essential support for $L^2$ transforms.

items.label: Paley–Wiener Theorem
items.statement: Compact frequency support corresponds to entire extension with controlled exponential growth.
items.formal_statement: For $f\in L^2(\mathbb R)$, $\operatorname{supp}\hat f\subset[-W,W]$ iff $f$ agrees a.e. on $\mathbb R$ with an entire function $F$ such that $F|_{\mathbb R}\in L^2(\mathbb R)$ and $|F(z)|\le C e^{2\pi W|\operatorname{Im}z|}$ in the appropriate $L^2$ Paley-Wiener sense.
items.definition: $$\begin{aligned} f\in L^2(\mathbb R),\ \operatorname{supp}\widehat f\subset[-W,W]&\Longleftrightarrow \exists F\in\mathcal O(\mathbb C):\ F|_{\mathbb R}=f\ \text{a.e.},\\ |F(z)|&\le Ce^{2\pi W|\operatorname{Im}z|},\\ F|_{\mathbb R}&\in L^2(\mathbb R). \end{aligned}$$
items.intuition: Cutting off frequency creates strong analytic structure in space. Band limitation is not just smoothness; it forces complex-analytic continuation.
assumptions: This is a schematic $L^2$ Paley-Wiener formulation; precise versions specify the exact growth and square-integrability condition.

items.label: Heat Kernel on R
items.statement: The Gaussian convolution kernel solving the heat equation on the real line.
items.formal_statement: For $t>0$, $H_t(x)=\frac1{\sqrt{4\pi t}}e^{-x^2/(4t)}$, $\hat H_t(\xi)=e^{-4\pi^2t\xi^2}$, and $u(x,t)=(H_t*f)(x)$ solves $u_t=u_{xx}$ with initial data $f$ in suitable senses.
items.intuition: Heat flow is Gaussian averaging. As $t\downarrow0$, $H_t$ concentrates near $0$; as $t$ grows, it spreads and smooths.
assumptions: The initial-data interpretation depends on the function space, such as $L^p$, $C_0$, or distributions.
items.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.5 Corollary 1.5; Ch.5 Corollary 3.4; Ch.5 Theorem 3.3

metadata.source: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.5 Corollary 1.5; Ch.5 Corollary 3.4; Ch.5 Theorem 3.3

items.label: Test Function
items.statement: A smooth compactly supported function used to probe distributions.
items.formal_statement: A test function is $\varphi\in C_c^\infty(\mathbb R)$. The space of test functions is often denoted $\mathcal D(\mathbb R)$, and $C_c^\infty(\mathbb R)\subset\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$.
items.intuition: Test functions are controlled measuring instruments. A distribution is known by how it acts on every such probe.

items.label: Convolution of Distributions
items.statement: Convolution can be extended to distributions when the support hypotheses make the pairing well-defined.
items.formal_statement: For a distribution $T$ and test function $\varphi$, $(T*\varphi)(x)=T(\tau_x\tilde\varphi)$, where $\tilde\varphi(y)=\varphi(-y)$. More generally, $T*S$ is defined under suitable support conditions, and $\delta*T=T$.
items.intuition: Convolution with a test function smooths a distribution. This lets spikes and generalized sources interact with kernels in PDEs.
assumptions: Convolution of two arbitrary distributions is not always defined; support or regularity hypotheses are required.

items.label: Fundamental Solution
items.statement: A distribution $E$ with $LE=\delta$, used to solve linear equations by convolution.
items.formal_statement: For a linear constant-coefficient differential operator $L$, a fundamental solution is a distribution $E$ satisfying $LE=\delta$. If convolution is defined, then $u=E*f$ solves $Lu=f$.
items.intuition: A fundamental solution is the response to a point source. Once the point-source response is known, general forcing is obtained by superposition through convolution.
assumptions: The formula $u=E*f$ requires convolution to be defined and $L$ to commute with convolution in the intended setting.

items.label: Parseval for the DFT
items.statement: Energy is conserved by the DFT up to the chosen normalization factor.
items.formal_statement: With $\hat a(k)=\sum_{j=0}^{N-1}a_j\omega_N^{-jk}$, $\sum_{j=0}^{N-1}|a_j|^2=\frac1N\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}|\hat a(k)|^2$.
items.intuition: The DFT matrix is unitary after scaling by $1/\sqrt N$. This is finite-dimensional Plancherel.
assumptions: Depends on the DFT normalization convention.

items.label: Discrete Cosine Transform
items.statement: A cosine-only transform obtained from an even extension of finite data.
items.formal_statement: One common DCT-II convention is $C_k=\sum_{j=0}^{N-1}a_j\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{N}(j+\frac12)k\right)$, $k=0,\dots,N-1$. It can be viewed as a DFT of a symmetrically extended sequence.
items.intuition: Even symmetry removes sine components. The DCT often concentrates signal energy into few low-frequency coefficients, which is why it is useful in compression.
assumptions: Several DCT normalizations and types exist; this item states the common DCT-II convention.

items.label: Separation of Variables
items.statement: A method for solving linear PDEs by expanding in spatial eigenfunctions.
items.formal_statement: Seek $u(x,t)=\sum_n c_n(t)e_n(x)$, where $e_n$ are eigenfunctions of the spatial operator. Orthogonality decouples the PDE into ODEs for the coefficients $c_n(t)$.
items.intuition: The method chooses coordinates that match the operator. In those coordinates, the PDE splits into independent one-frequency motions.
assumptions: Requires a suitable eigenfunction expansion for the spatial problem.

items.label: Dirichlet Problem on the Disk
items.statement: The Poisson kernel gives the harmonic extension of boundary data on the disk.
items.formal_statement: If $f\in C(\mathbb T)$, then $u(r,\theta)=(P_r*f)(\theta)$ is harmonic for $0\le r<1$ and $u(r,\theta)\to f(\theta)$ as $r\to1^-$.
items.intuition: Boundary values are averaged with a kernel that concentrates near the boundary point as one approaches the circle.
assumptions: Boundary convergence is stated for continuous boundary data; weaker data require weaker convergence modes.

items.label: Theta Function & Functional Equation
items.statement: Poisson summation applied to the Gaussian gives the modular identity for the theta function.
items.formal_statement: For $t>0$, $\theta(t)=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}e^{-\pi n^2t}$ satisfies $\theta(1/t)=\sqrt t\,\theta(t)$.
items.intuition: The Gaussian is essentially fixed by the Fourier transform, so Poisson summation turns a sum at scale $t$ into a sum at reciprocal scale $1/t$.
assumptions: Uses the $2\pi$-Fourier convention where $e^{-\pi x^2}$ is self-dual.

items.label: Free Schrödinger Equation
items.statement: The free Schrödinger equation is solved by multiplying each Fourier mode by a dispersive phase.
items.formal_statement: For $iu_t=-u_{xx}$ on $\mathbb R$ with initial data $u_0$, the Fourier transform satisfies $\hat u(\xi,t)=e^{-4\pi^2 i\xi^2t}\hat u_0(\xi)$.
items.intuition: The Fourier transform turns the spatial second derivative into multiplication by $-4\pi^2\xi^2$. Unlike heat flow, the multiplier has modulus one, so waves disperse rather than decay.
assumptions: Uses the Fourier convention $e^{-2\pi ix\xi}$. Regularity assumptions depend on whether one wants classical, $L^2$, or distributional solutions.

items.label: Fraunhofer Diffraction
items.statement: In the Fraunhofer far-field approximation, the diffraction amplitude is essentially the Fourier transform of the aperture.
items.formal_statement: For an aperture indicator $\mathbf 1_A$ in the aperture plane, the far-field amplitude is proportional to $\widehat{\mathbf 1_A}(\xi)$, and the observed intensity is proportional to $|\widehat{\mathbf 1_A}(\xi)|^2$.
items.intuition: A lens or far-field propagation physically separates spatial frequencies. A slit produces a sinc pattern; a circular aperture produces an Airy-type pattern.
assumptions: This is the scalar Fraunhofer approximation, not a full electromagnetic model.

items.label: Weierstrass Function (Nowhere Differentiable)
items.statement: A continuous nowhere-differentiable periodic function can be built from a lacunary trigonometric series.
items.formal_statement: A classical example is $W(x)=\sum_{n=0}^\infty a^n\cos(b^n x)$, where $0<a<1$, $b$ is an odd integer, and suitable conditions such as $ab>1+3\pi/2$ guarantee that $W$ is continuous and nowhere differentiable.
items.intuition: The coefficients decay enough to give continuity, but the rapidly separated frequencies keep injecting small sharp oscillations at every scale.
assumptions: The displayed condition is a classical sufficient condition, not the sharpest possible theorem.
items.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.4 Lemma 3.2; Ch.4 Lemma 3.3; Ch.4 Theorem 3.1

metadata.source: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.4 Lemma 3.2; Ch.4 Lemma 3.3; Ch.4 Theorem 3.1

items.label: Poisson Kernel on the Half-Plane
items.statement: The Poisson kernel solving the Dirichlet problem on the upper half-plane.
items.formal_statement: For $y>0$, $P_y(x)=\frac1\pi\frac{y}{x^2+y^2}$, $\hat P_y(\xi)=e^{-2\pi y|\xi|}$, and $u(x,y)=(f*P_y)(x)$ is harmonic in $\mathbb R^2_+$ with boundary trace $f$ under suitable hypotheses.
items.intuition: It is the real-line version of harmonic extension: moving upward into the half-plane smooths boundary data by a scale $y$.
assumptions: Boundary convergence depends on the function space of $f$.
items.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.5 Lemma 2.4; Ch.5 Lemma 2.5; Ch.5 Lemma 2.8; Ch.5 Theorem 2.6; Ch.5 Theorem 2.7; Ch.5 Theorem 3.5

items.label: Wave Equation in R^d (Huygens, Kirchhoff)
items.statement: The multidimensional wave equation is solved in frequency by independent oscillators.
items.formal_statement: For $\partial_t^2u=\Delta u$ with $u(\cdot,0)=f$, $u_t(\cdot,0)=g$, the Fourier transform gives $\hat u(\xi,t)=\cos(2\pi|\xi|t)\hat f(\xi)+\frac{\sin(2\pi|\xi|t)}{2\pi|\xi|}\hat g(\xi)$, with the second term interpreted by its limit at $\xi=0$.
items.intuition: Each frequency oscillates at speed proportional to $|\xi|$. Special odd-dimensional formulas reveal Huygens' principle, where waves propagate sharply without a trailing wake.
assumptions: Formula uses the $2\pi$-frequency convention and requires suitable data or distributional interpretation.

items.label: Radon Transform & Tomography
items.statement: The Radon transform reconstructs a function from its integrals over hyperplanes.
items.formal_statement: For suitable $f$ on $\mathbb R^d$, $Rf(t,\gamma)=\int_{x\cdot\gamma=t}f(x)\,d\sigma(x)$. The Fourier slice theorem says the one-dimensional Fourier transform in $t$ satisfies $\mathcal F_t(Rf)(s,\gamma)=\hat f(s\gamma)$.
items.intuition: Each X-ray projection reveals one slice of the Fourier transform. Combining slices over many directions reconstructs the object.
assumptions: Requires suitable decay or integrability so the hyperplane integrals and transforms are defined.
items.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.6 Corollary 5.3; Ch.6 Lemma 5.2; Ch.6 Proposition 5.1; Ch.6 Theorem 5.4

items.label: Dual Group
items.statement: The group of all characters of a finite abelian group.
items.formal_statement: For a finite abelian group $G$, its dual group is $\widehat G=\operatorname{Hom}(G,\mathbb C^\times)$, with pointwise multiplication. The group $\widehat G$ is finite abelian and is isomorphic to $G$, though not canonically in general.
items.intuition: The dual group is the frequency side of $G$. Its elements are the waves that detect structure in functions on $G$.
assumptions: For finite groups, characters take values among roots of unity.

items.label: Characters of Finite Abelian Groups
items.statement: Characters form an orthonormal basis for functions on a finite abelian group.
items.formal_statement: Let $G$ be a finite abelian group and use $\langle f,g\rangle=\frac1{|G|}\sum_{x\in G}f(x)\overline{g(x)}$. Then the characters $\chi\in\widehat G$ form an orthonormal basis of $L^2(G)$. Hence $\hat f(\chi)=\frac1{|G|}\sum_{x\in G}f(x)\overline{\chi(x)}$ and $f(x)=\sum_{\chi\in\widehat G}\hat f(\chi)\chi(x)$.
items.intuition: Every finite abelian group has enough pure frequencies to decompose every function on it. The cyclic DFT is just the special case $G=\mathbb Z/N\mathbb Z$.
assumptions: The group is finite and abelian. Nonabelian Fourier analysis requires representations, not just scalar characters.
items.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.7 Lemma 2.2; Ch.7 Lemma 2.4; Ch.7 Lemma 2.6; Ch.7 Theorem 2.3; Ch.7 Theorem 2.5; Ch.7 Theorem 2.7; Ch.7 Theorem 2.8

items.label: Euclidean Algorithm & Bézout
items.statement: The Euclidean algorithm computes greatest common divisors and gives Bézout identities.
items.formal_statement: For integers $a,b$ with $b>0$, there are unique integers $q,r$ such that $a=qb+r$ and $0\le r<b$. Iterating division gives $\gcd(a,b)$, and there exist integers $m,n$ such that $\gcd(a,b)=ma+nb$.
items.definition: $$\begin{aligned} a,b&\in\mathbb Z,\\ b>0&\Longrightarrow \exists q,r\in\mathbb Z:\ a=qb+r,\quad 0\le r<b,\\ \gcd(a,b)&=ma+nb\quad(m,n\in\mathbb Z). \end{aligned}$$
items.intuition: Repeatedly subtract the largest possible multiple until no remainder remains. Running the steps backward expresses the gcd as an integer linear combination.
assumptions: Usually stated for integers $a,b$, not both zero.
items.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.8 Theorem 1.1; Ch.8 Theorem 1.2; Ch.8 Corollary 1.3; Ch.8 Corollary 1.4

metadata.source: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.8 Theorem 1.1; Ch.8 Theorem 1.2; Ch.8 Corollary 1.3; Ch.8 Corollary 1.4

items.label: Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic
items.statement: Every integer greater than $1$ factors uniquely into primes.
items.formal_statement: For every integer $n>1$, there exist primes $p_1<\cdots<p_k$ and positive integers $\alpha_1,\dots,\alpha_k$ such that $n=p_1^{\alpha_1}\cdots p_k^{\alpha_k}$. This factorization is unique.
items.intuition: Primes are the multiplicative atoms of the integers. Unique factorization lets analytic sums over integers become products over primes.
assumptions: Units and ordering are ignored: uniqueness means the same primes occur with the same exponents.

items.label: Infinitude of Primes
items.statement: There are infinitely many primes.
items.formal_statement: If $p_1,\dots,p_k$ are finitely many primes, then any prime divisor of $p_1\cdots p_k+1$ is not among $p_1,\dots,p_k$. Hence no finite list contains all primes.
items.intuition: Any alleged complete list of primes manufactures a new number that escapes divisibility by every prime on the list.

items.label: Euler Product for the Zeta Function
items.statement: The zeta function factors into local contributions from each prime.
items.formal_statement: For $\operatorname{Re}s>1$, $\zeta(s)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}n^{-s}=\prod_{p}(1-p^{-s})^{-1}$, with absolute convergence justifying the rearrangement.
items.intuition: Unique factorization turns the sum over all integers into independent geometric series over primes. This is where arithmetic becomes analysis.
assumptions: Initially valid only in the half-plane $\operatorname{Re}s>1$.
items.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.8 Lemma 1.8; Ch.8 Proposition 1.9; Ch.8 Theorem 1.10; Ch.8 Proposition 3.2

metadata.source: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.8 Lemma 1.8; Ch.8 Proposition 1.9; Ch.8 Theorem 1.10; Ch.8 Proposition 3.2

items.label: Divergence of the Prime Sum
items.statement: The reciprocal sum over the primes diverges.
items.formal_statement: $\sum_p\frac1p=\infty$. One proof takes logarithms of the Euler product and compares $\log\zeta(s)$ as $s\to1^+$.
items.intuition: There are not merely infinitely many primes; they are numerous enough that their reciprocal weights still accumulate without bound.
assumptions: The theorem is stronger than Euclid’s infinitude of primes.

items.label: Residue Class Modulo q
items.statement: Integers are grouped by having the same remainder modulo $q$.
items.formal_statement: For $q\ge2$, $a\equiv b\pmod q$ means $q\mid(a-b)$. The residue class of $a$ is $[a]_q=\{n\in\mathbb Z:n\equiv a\pmod q\}$.
items.definition: $$a\equiv b\pmod q\Longleftrightarrow q\mid(a-b),\qquad [a]_q=\{n\in\mathbb Z:n\equiv a\pmod q\}.$$
items.intuition: A residue class is one lane in modular arithmetic. Dirichlet’s theorem asks whether each reduced lane contains infinitely many primes.

items.label: Euler Phi Function
items.statement: Euler’s phi function counts the invertible residue classes modulo $q$.
items.formal_statement: $\varphi(q)=| (\mathbb Z/q\mathbb Z)^\times |=|\{1\le a\le q:\gcd(a,q)=1\}|$.
items.intuition: The number $\varphi(q)$ is the size of the frequency system used by Dirichlet characters modulo $q$.

items.label: Riemann Zeta Function
items.statement: The Dirichlet series whose Euler product encodes the primes.
items.formal_statement: For $\operatorname{Re}s>1$, $\zeta(s)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}n^{-s}$. It has Euler product $\zeta(s)=\prod_p(1-p^{-s})^{-1}$.
items.intuition: The zeta function packages all positive integers into one analytic object; unique factorization then rewrites it as a product over primes.
assumptions: The displayed Dirichlet series and Euler product are valid for $\operatorname{Re}s>1$.

items.label: Dirichlet Character
items.statement: A Dirichlet character is a group character modulo $q$, extended by zero on non-coprime integers.
items.formal_statement: Modulo $q$, a Dirichlet character $\chi:\mathbb Z\to\mathbb C$ is $q$-periodic, completely multiplicative, agrees with a character on $(\mathbb Z/q\mathbb Z)^\times$, and satisfies $\chi(n)=0$ when $\gcd(n,q)>1$. For $\gcd(a,q)=1$, $\frac1{\varphi(q)}\sum_{\chi\bmod q}\overline{\chi(a)}\chi(n)$ detects the reduced residue class $n\equiv a\pmod q$.
items.intuition: Dirichlet characters are multiplicative Fourier modes for reduced residue classes. They convert congruence conditions into character sums.
assumptions: Characters are extended by zero away from integers coprime to $q$.

items.label: Dirichlet L-Function
items.statement: A Dirichlet L-function is the zeta function twisted by a Dirichlet character.
items.formal_statement: For $\operatorname{Re}s>1$, $L(s,\chi)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\chi(n)n^{-s}=\prod_p(1-\chi(p)p^{-s})^{-1}$. For a nonprincipal character, $L(s,\chi)$ has a finite value at $s=1$ by analytic continuation or Abel limiting.
items.intuition: The character twist filters integers, and especially primes, by residue class. The behavior near $s=1$ is the analytic lever in Dirichlet’s theorem.
assumptions: Euler product identity is initially valid for $\operatorname{Re}s>1$. The principal character gives a zeta-like pole at $s=1$.
items.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.8 Theorem 2.4; Ch.8 Proposition 3.1; Ch.8 Proposition 3.4; Ch.8 Lemma 3.5; Ch.8 Proposition 3.6

items.label: Non-Vanishing of L(1,χ)
items.statement: Nonprincipal Dirichlet L-functions do not vanish at $s=1$.
items.formal_statement: If $\chi$ is a nonprincipal Dirichlet character modulo $q$, then $L(1,\chi)\ne0$.
items.intuition: The character decomposition of residue classes would fail to force primes in every class if these values could vanish. Non-vanishing blocks that cancellation.
assumptions: The principal character is excluded; its $L$-function has a pole at $s=1$, not a finite nonzero value.

items.label: Essential Supremum
items.statement: The almost-everywhere version of a maximum size.
items.formal_statement: For a measurable function $f$, $\operatorname*{ess\,sup}|f|$ is the least $M\in[0,\infty]$ such that $|f(x)|\le M$ for almost every $x$.
items.intuition: It ignores values on sets of measure zero, so it is the correct maximum-size notion for $L^\infty$.

items.label: L^1 on R
items.statement: Absolutely integrable functions on the real line.
items.formal_statement: $L^1(\mathbb R)=\{f:\int_{\mathbb R}|f(x)|\,dx<\infty\}$, with functions identified if they agree almost everywhere.
items.definition: $$\begin{aligned} L^1(\mathbb R)&=\left\{f:\mathbb R\to\mathbb C\ \text{measurable}:\int_{\mathbb R}|f(x)|\,dx<\infty\right\}/\sim,\\ f\sim g&\Longleftrightarrow f=g\ \text{a.e.} \end{aligned}$$
items.intuition: This is the natural minimal setting where the Fourier transform integral is defined as an absolutely convergent integral.
assumptions: Functions are identified up to equality almost everywhere.

items.label: L^2 on R
items.statement: Square-integrable functions on the real line.
items.formal_statement: $L^2(\mathbb R)=\{f:\int_{\mathbb R}|f(x)|^2\,dx<\infty\}$, modulo equality almost everywhere, with inner product $\langle f,g\rangle=\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)\overline{g(x)}\,dx$.
items.definition: $$\begin{aligned} L^2(\mathbb R)&=\left\{f:\mathbb R\to\mathbb C\ \text{measurable}:\int_{\mathbb R}|f(x)|^2\,dx<\infty\right\}/\sim,\\ f\sim g&\Longleftrightarrow f=g\ \text{a.e.},\\ \langle f,g\rangle&=\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)\overline{g(x)}\,dx. \end{aligned}$$
items.intuition: This is the energy space for the Fourier transform. Plancherel says the transform preserves this energy exactly.
assumptions: Functions are identified up to equality almost everywhere.

items.label: Support
items.statement: The region where a function or distribution is nonzero.
items.formal_statement: For a continuous function, $\operatorname{supp}f=\overline{\{x:f(x)\ne0\}}$. For measurable functions, support is usually understood up to almost-everywhere changes.
items.intuition: Support tells where an object lives. Frequency support tells which waves are present.

items.label: Sinc Function
items.statement: The interpolation kernel in the sampling theorem.
items.formal_statement: $\operatorname{sinc}(t)=\frac{\sin(\pi t)}{\pi t}$ for $t\ne0$, with $\operatorname{sinc}(0)=1$.
items.intuition: Sinc is the ideal reconstruction pulse: it equals one at its own sample point and zero at the other integer sample points.
assumptions: This graph uses the normalized sinc convention $\operatorname{sinc}(t)=\sin(\pi t)/(\pi t)$.

items.label: Entire Function
items.statement: A complex function holomorphic on the whole complex plane.
items.formal_statement: A function $F:\mathbb C\to\mathbb C$ is entire if it is complex-differentiable at every point of $\mathbb C$.
items.intuition: Entire functions are globally analytic. Paley-Wiener connects compact frequency support with this strong complex-analytic regularity.
assumptions: This is a complex-analysis prerequisite used only where analytic continuation matters.

items.label: Fourier Transform Convention
items.statement: The sign and 2π-normalization used for the Fourier transform.
items.formal_statement: $\hat f(\xi)=\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,dx$, with inverse $f(x)=\int_{\mathbb R}\hat f(\xi)e^{2\pi i x\xi}\,d\xi$ when inversion is valid.
items.definition: $$\widehat f(\xi)=\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)e^{-2\pi ix\xi}\,dx,\qquad f(x)=\int_{\mathbb R}\widehat f(\xi)e^{2\pi ix\xi}\,d\xi.$$
items.intuition: Fourier constants are bookkeeping with teeth. Fixing the convention prevents sign and 2π errors across inversion, Plancherel, PDEs, and uncertainty.

items.label: Variance and Standard Deviation
items.statement: The centered second moment measuring spread.
items.formal_statement: For a normalized density $\rho$, $\mu=\int x\rho(x)\,dx$ and $\Delta_x^2=\int (x-\mu)^2\rho(x)\,dx$. In uncertainty estimates, $\rho(x)=|f(x)|^2/\|f\|_2^2$.
items.intuition: The uncertainty principle is about spread around the center of mass, not just raw distance from zero.
